text: The robotic sigmoid resection reveals inflammatory adhesions of the omentum attached to the tumor, which are left in place during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, carefully staying on the back of the mesorectum while protecting the nerves. The ureter is identified and gently swept down during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node at the junction of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and the aorta, resecting it as a separate specimen. The surgeon then clears tissue from the IMA, focusing on the area distal to its aortic takeoff and proximal to the left colic artery.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, progressing laterally while identifying the gonadal vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) has been taken to improve reach and lymph node yield.

I really think that we should all make attempts to go as far in this plane as we can before going to lateral because it allows the lateral dissection to go a lot faster. And you'll see that right here. just with one incision, you've joined your medial plane. I have no concerns about the ureter because my sponge is over top of it.
The surgeon separates the sigmoid mesentery from the sidewall, noting that only a single cell layer remains holding it in place. The splenic flexure is not mobilized during this procedure.

The surgeon clears off the rectosigmoid mesentery, preparing for an intracorporeal sewn anastomosis. The proximal mesentery is taken, ensuring adequate length for the procedure. The specimen will be exteriorized through a fan and steel incision.

Many of you have commented on watching some of my videos in the past about concerns about the type of anastomosis that you'll see me start here and the little lip of bowel between the staple line and myotomy becoming ischemic and whether or not that has a clinical importance. And although I see what many of you are talking about, it has never been anything that I have been concerned about. And I'll show in a little bit why I feel that way.

The surgeon performs proximal and distal division, aligning the anastomosis by sewing the posterior outer row with a V-lock suture. The otomies are made on a well-vascularized rectum, with attention to the intervening segment between the staple line and the otomy, which may appear ischemic.

The surgeon observes the rectal tissue, noting that most of the time it enhances with ICG and remains pink and perfused. However, in this case, there is a noticeable discrepancy in the ICG enhancement, with some areas appearing dusky.
The surgeon amputates the staple line joining two bowel segments and converts it to an end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring the bowel is healthy and well-perfused.
The surgeon aligns the bowel segments for an end-to-end anastomosis, noting improved perfusion. They proceed to sew a single layer anastomosis.

The surgeon places a trio silk seromuscular suture posteriorly in the midline to align the bowel segments, sewing towards the left side. The bowel appears healthy with a wide open lumen and no tension.

And now we'll switch to a canal to come over the top. I used to be exclusively a two layer sewn anastomosis surgeon. I have just in the last probably year adopted single layer anastomosis and I have to say I'm very happy with it.

Reasons why I don't do this more often is particularly when I have stapled ends of the colon. I just don't necessarily like taking the time to cut the staple lines off. It kind of feels like a waste of the stapler, but in a cancer operation, I do prefer to staple the segment of a specimen so that there's theoretically no tumor shedding during bowel manipulation if I had left that segment with open ends. There are dozens of very appropriate and safe ways to do this same operation.

This is just currently the way that I prefer to do the operation, and I encourage all of us to just do what they are most comfortable with. Again, the teaching point for myself is despite your experience level, despite your skill set, I think that at any point of an operation, if it doesn't look absolutely perfect and you have an opportunity to fix it,

I encourage you to do that before you leave the operating room, particularly in anastomosis. It pains me to know that in situations like this, or maybe even if there was an air leak on testing, that some patients might end up getting a diverting stoma. the solution to the problem. And I just think that if you have room to revise your anastomosis, even if it takes you that extra half an hour or an hour, that is what is best for the patient.

The surgeon uses a suture to canal over the top towards the patient's right, bringing the two sutures towards the middle. The mucosa is imbricated so that only the serosa is visible touching serosa. The surgeon goes back and forth to ensure proper closure without bowing or folding, particularly focusing on the corners. Additional 3-0 silk sutures are added as needed.

The surgeon uses invocating sutures to improve the technique, ensuring the lip of the tissue is securely dunked and completely imbricated. The sutures are passed back and forth to confirm the lip is secure, and the two ends are tied together.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, observing no tension in the tissue. Irrigant is introduced posteriorly to release any trapped air bubbles, ensuring the integrity of the anastomosis.
